name: Rattlesnake Butte
short_description: Rockhound the high plains of northwest South Dakota for colorful agates, jasper, and petrified wood. Flat, accessible terrain near the Grand River makes this an excellent beginner-friendly site.
description:
Rattlesnake Butte sits in the remote high plains of Ziebach County, where the landscape opens into rolling prairie interspersed with badland formations and scattered buttes. This area has long been known to local rockhounders as a productive site for agate, chalcedony, and colorful jasper specimens weathered from Tertiary-age volcanic and sedimentary deposits. The butte itself rises modestly above the surrounding grassland, offering decent views across the Grand River valley and access to washes and hillsides where specimens collect naturally.

The collecting area is relatively flat and open, making it accessible to rockhounders of all experience levels. Specimens tend to be scattered across wash beds and eroded slopes rather than concentrated in dense pockets, so you will need to walk and search methodically. Most productive collecting happens after spring runoff or summer storms, when new material is exposed.

This is prime remote backcountry rockhounding in South Dakota's least developed county. Cell service is spotty or absent, and the nearest services are 30+ miles away, so plan accordingly with fuel, water, and supplies. The high plains environment is wind-swept and exposed, with minimal shade, making sun protection and hydration critical even in cooler months.

Because the exact ownership and access status of some parcels near Rattlesnake Butte can be unclear, always verify land status before collecting. Much of the surrounding land is either BLM or private ranch. If collecting on BLM land, the standard personal-use limits apply.
history: Rattlesnake Butte and the surrounding Ziebach County high plains were shaped by Tertiary-age volcanic activity and the deposition of ash and silica-rich sediments 50 to 20 million years ago. Chalcedony, agate, and jasper formed within these volcanic and clastic sequences, and subsequent weathering and erosion by the Grand River and its tributaries has exposed and concentrated specimens in accessible washes and hillside slopes. Early settlers and ranchers in the region have long collected specimens here; modern rockhounders continue to find quality material across the public and open private lands.
tips: Search wash beds and the exposed banks of small drainages on and around the butte slopes, where water erosion naturally concentrates specimens. Look for milky white, translucent, or banded agate nodules and pieces of colorful jasper and chalcedony in the size range of 0.5 to 3 inches. Bring a small backpack, sturdy boots, sunscreen, and at least 3 liters of water; shade and facilities are nonexistent. Visit after spring snowmelt or summer storms when recent erosion exposes fresh material.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling high plains terrain at approximately 2,400 feet elevation. Short prairie grass and scattered sagebrush with occasional rocky outcrops and wash beds. The butte itself provides moderate slopes and some exposed clay and sandstone. Walking is generally easy on open ground, but footing can be uneven and rocky in washes. Minimal vegetation means full sun exposure and wind. Most productive collecting is within 1 to 3 miles of the main butte feature.
best_season: April - October; avoid winter snow and cold. Peak season is May - June (post-spring runoff) and September - October (cooler temperatures). Summer (July-August) is hot and dry; early spring can be muddy.
written_directions: From Lemmon, South Dakota (the closest town with services), head south on SD 73 for approximately 12 miles. Turn east on county road and continue 8-10 miles toward the Ziebach County area. GPS coordinates will take you directly to Rattlesnake Butte. Road conditions vary from paved highway to unpaved county and ranch roads. Check road status locally before driving, especially after storms or snowmelt. High-clearance vehicle recommended for final approach.
road_conditions: Mix of paved and unpaved county roads. Final approach roads may be washboard or rough during wet conditions. Standard passenger vehicles can usually reach the area, but high-clearance recommended.
parking_notes: Informal parking areas near the butte base. No developed parking lot. Pull well off active ranch roads and respect any gates. Park where you can be seen from a distance in this remote area.
collection_rules: Verify land ownership before collecting. If on BLM land: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No large-scale digging or hammering. If on private ranch land: Obtain permission from the landowner before collecting anything.
quantity_limits: 25 lbs per day (BLM land only); confirm ownership first
rules:
Personal use only on public land
Maximum 25 lbs per day on BLM
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No large hammering or powdering of outcrops
Verify land ownership before collecting
Obtain landowner permission on private property
Respect any posted boundaries or gates
nearest_services: Lemmon, South Dakota (approximately 20 miles north). Limited gas, food, and water. No services or facilities at the site itself.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat and open terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven, rocky wash beds may challenge those with limited mobility. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Extreme remoteness, lack of shade, and full sun exposure make this challenging for very young children or those sensitive to heat and wind.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Rattlesnake Butte in South Dakota?
faq.answer: Rattlesnake Butte is known for colorful agate, chalcedony, jasper, and petrified wood. Most specimens range from 0.5 to 3 inches and are found scattered in wash beds and along eroded hillsides. Agate nodules often feature banding or translucent sections, while jasper typically shows reds, oranges, and earth tones.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Rattlesnake Butte free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Rattlesnake Butte is free if you collect on BLM public land, which makes up much of the accessible area. However, you must verify land ownership before collecting, as some nearby parcels are private ranch land. Always check with local authorities or obtain landowner permission on private property before taking specimens.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Rattlesnake Butte, South Dakota?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on BLM land at Rattlesnake Butte. However, you must follow federal regulations: maximum 25 lbs per day, personal use only, and no commercial collection or motorized excavation. Always verify land ownership first, as private land requires permission.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Rattlesnake Butte?
faq.answer: The best seasons are May - June (after spring runoff exposes fresh material) and September - October (cooler temperatures). April through October is generally good, while winter brings snow and cold. Avoid July - August due to extreme heat and dry conditions on the exposed high plains.

faq.question: Is Rattlesnake Butte a good rockhounding location for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Rattlesnake Butte is beginner-friendly because the terrain is flat and open, specimens are visible, and no special techniques are required. However, the extreme remoteness, lack of cell service, intense sun, and distance from services make it better suited for rockhounders who are prepared for backcountry conditions. Bring water, sunscreen, and tell someone your plans.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Rattlesnake Butte in Ziebach County?
faq.answer: Bring a backpack, sturdy hiking boots, at least 3 liters of water, sunscreen, a hat, and a light jacket for wind. A small pick or pry bar can help extract specimens from clay banks, but heavy hammering is not allowed. Because you are 20+ miles from the nearest town and cell service is nonexistent, carry a map, fully charge all devices before going, and tell someone your plans.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Rattlesnake Butte, SD. Free BLM rockhounding on remote high plains. Beginner-friendly. No permit needed.
